---
title: Stroke reorganises the transient dynamical structure of human gait
authors:
  - name: Michael Hess
    affiliation: Emory University, Department of Biology
    roles: writing
    corresponding: true
    email: mhess3@emory.edu
  - name: Gordon Berman
    affiliation: Emory University, Departments of Biology & Physics
    roles: writing
    corresponding: false
bibliography: references.bib
jupyter: julia-1.12
engine: julia
# julia.exeflags: ["--threads", "auto"]
---

::: {.callout-note}
Project navigation: [Weekly updates](weekly_updates/) | [Manuscript PDF](index.pdf)
:::

## Abstract
Healthy walking absorbs perturbations across a range of frequencies and directions, integrating rhythmic activity across both limbs and multiple joints. Classical measures of gait stability -- Floquet multipliers and the maximum Lyapunov exponent -- summarise this bilateral, multi-joint coordination with a single number describing how fast perturbations grow or decay, and by construction cannot report which directions matter, at which frequencies the system responds, or how the two limbs couple to one another. Here we show that fitting a simple linear model to walking kinematics and examining its geometry -- rather than only its growth rates -- recovers these missing aspects of coordination directly from data. Applied to treadmill walking trials from able-bodied and post-stroke participants, three properties of the fitted model capture distinct features of gait: how broadly the system can amplify transient perturbations, how wide a band of frequencies it can respond to, and how symmetrically the two limbs drive one another. These properties jointly separate able-bodied, high-functioning post-stroke, and low-functioning post-stroke gait into distinct coordination regimes. Able-bodied gait is broadly responsive and symmetric across limbs; high-functioning stroke gait concentrates its response sharply at stride frequency, achieving strong rhythmic resonance but narrow frequency breadth; low-functioning stroke gait is weakly responsive across the spectrum. Both stroke groups channel inter-limb coupling through the non-paretic knee, and speed-dependent changes in coordination dimensionality correlate with clinical balance function. These results suggest that post-stroke locomotor impairment is not a uniform degradation of healthy gait along a single axis, but a structured reorganisation of coordination that takes different forms in different functional groups -- and that the geometry of a data-driven model of walking, not just its stability, carries biological information.

## Introduction

Walking looks simple but requires remarkably sophisticated coordination. Each stride involves dozens of muscles acting across multiple joints on both legs, timed to maintain balance, absorb ground reaction forces, and propel the body forward. When this coordination is disrupted by neurological injury such as stroke, the consequences are visible not just as slower walking or asymmetric steps, but as a qualitative change in how the whole system responds to the moment-to-moment perturbations -- from uneven ground, shifting attention, or the body's own variability -- that healthy walkers handle without conscious effort.

How should we measure this coordination? The dominant approach treats walking as a periodic orbit and asks a single question: do perturbations shrink or grow? Floquet multipliers answer this per stride cycle, but require strict periodicity and very large datasets -- @revzen2008 needed over 10,000 strides from cockroach running data just to obtain stable estimates, orders of magnitude more than the ~15--50 strides in a typical human trial. They also demand a reliable phase estimate, itself a hard problem [@revzen2012; @bruijn2013]. The maximum Lyapunov exponent ($\lambda_S$) relaxes the periodicity assumption and has emerged as the best-validated predictor of fall risk [@dingwell2000; @bruijn2013], but it provides only a scalar: how fast do nearby trajectories pull apart? It says nothing about *which directions* are unstable, *at what frequencies* the system can respond, or *how the two limbs talk to each other*. These are precisely the questions that matter for understanding locomotor coordination after stroke.

Both measures also share a deeper conceptual issue: they assume the system is autonomous -- evolving under its own dynamics without external input. Walking is not autonomous. It is continuously driven by descending neural commands, modulated by sensory feedback, and stabilised by reflexes [@ting2007]. The common finding that $\lambda_S$ is positive (local divergence) despite stable walking is a direct consequence: the system is being simultaneously driven and controlled, and Lyapunov analysis of the autonomous reconstruction picks up the driving, not a genuine instability [@dingwell2006].

We take a different approach. Rather than asking a scalar stability question, we fit a simple linear model to the walking data and then examine the *structure of that model* to learn about the underlying coordination. Specifically, we use dynamic mode decomposition (DMD) -- a data-driven technique that finds the best-fit linear operator $A$ predicting the next time step from the current one [@schmid2010; @tu2014] -- applied to delay-embedded bilateral joint kinematics. Delay embedding stacks successive time points into a higher-dimensional state vector, allowing the linear model to capture temporal dependencies that a single snapshot cannot [@takens1981; @brunton2017].

Like Floquet and Lyapunov analysis, DMD fits an autonomous model. But rather than reducing the result to eigenvalues (which determine long-run stability), we examine the model's *non-normality*: how far the fitted operator departs from having orthogonal eigenvectors. This departure has concrete physical meaning. A “well-behaved” (normal) operator can only respond to perturbations through its individual modes, each decaying independently. A non-normal operator can transiently amplify specific perturbation directions, respond at frequencies where no single mode is resonant, and couple inputs and outputs in directionally specific ways [@trefethen2005; @henrici1962]. These are not abstract mathematical properties -- they map directly onto the locomotor questions we care about: How flexibly can the system respond to perturbations across frequencies? How do the two limbs influence each other? And does the system's coordination structure depend on walking speed, or is it robust across operating conditions?

Non-normality has previously been studied in the DMD literature primarily as a *numerical problem*: @wu2021 showed that when DMD is applied to oscillatory data, the underlying operator is inherently non-normal, leading to ill-conditioning and noise-amplified errors. We invert this perspective. Rather than treating non-normality as a nuisance, we show that its magnitude and structure -- recovered from walking kinematics -- are biologically informative signatures of locomotor coordination. The more complex the underlying dynamics that the linear operator must encode, the more non-normal eigenvector geometry it must deploy to do so. We term this encoding demand "dynamical strain" ($\eta$, quantified as the Henrici departure from normality): a measure of how hard the linear model must work to represent the nonlinear, multi-timescale coordination underlying each walking trial.

This framing also connects directly to the recent healthy-cohort study of @winner2024, which showed two key results across a wide speed range: (i) gait signatures remain individual-specific, and (ii) their speed modulation is approximately linear in a shared low-dimensional manifold. Those findings give us a healthy reference template: in intact gait, speed changes appear structured rather than chaotic, and the underlying coordination seems to remain on a common manifold as operating conditions change.

Post-stroke gait provides the critical perturbation of that template. The clinical picture includes slower walking speeds, inter-limb asymmetry, and elevated stride-to-stride variability [@bruijn2013], but these descriptions remain at the level of symptoms. A deeper question is whether stroke fundamentally reorganises *how* the locomotor system coordinates itself -- not just how fast or how symmetrically it walks, but the structure of its perturbation responses, its frequency content, and its inter-limb coupling. We test this hypothesis using 299 treadmill walking trials from the open dataset of @winner2023, which includes able-bodied controls and high- and low-functioning stroke survivors. Our approach complements their LSTM-based “gait signatures” framework: where that work used black-box neural networks to identify individual-specific dynamics, we use an explicitly analysable linear operator to extract interpretable coordination signatures. The central question, then, is not simply whether stroke gait is “worse,” but which operator-level regularities of healthy walking break first -- speed-invariance of the encoding demand, broadband spectral support, or bilateral coupling symmetry.

Throughout, we interpret five operator-level features. Dynamical strain ($\eta$) measures how strongly the fitted operator departs from orthogonal modal geometry, and therefore how much transiently amplifying, directionally coupled structure the linear model must deploy to encode the gait data. Harmonic resolvent gains measure how strongly that operator would amplify periodic forcing at stride-related frequencies, so biomechanically they index whether coordination is broad-band and multi-timescale or tightly locked to the stride rhythm. Spatial resolvent asymmetry measures whether perturbation pathways are exchanged symmetrically between limbs or preferentially routed in one direction. The Q-factor measures how narrow that stride-frequency tuning is. Finally, retained rank and participation ratio measure how many effective dynamical directions remain available for distributing that response across joints and timescales.

## Results

```{julia}
#| output: false
# Suppress stdout and all log messages (warnings, info) for all figure cells.
using Logging
_silent(f) = redirect_stdout(devnull) do
    with_logger(NullLogger()) do
        f()
    end
end
```

### Figure 0: Hankel DMD Pipeline from Joint Kinematics to Interpretable Operator Metrics


```{julia}
#| label: fig-pipeline
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Schematic overview of the Hankel DMD pipeline used throughout the paper. (A) Example bilateral joint-angle time series from one walking trial. (B) Delay embedding converts those six channels into a Hankel matrix whose columns stack successive frames, so each state contains both spatial and temporal gait context. (C) A reduced DMD operator is fit to the embedded state sequence, and the biologically relevant quantities are then read from the operator's non-normal geometry and resolvent structure rather than from individual eigenvalues alone. (D) Example short-horizon predictions from the fitted operator, illustrating that the model captures the local evolution of the gait waveform well enough to support interpretable operator-level analysis."
#| output: true

_silent(() -> include("Figure 0.jl"))
plot(fig0; fmt=:png)
```

Figure 0 shows the analysis logic at a glance. We begin with six bilateral joint-angle traces, convert them into a delay-embedded state so that each column carries a short window of gait history, fit a reduced linear operator to the resulting sequence, and then interpret that operator through quantities that have direct biomechanical meaning. In this paper, the key point is that we do not treat single eigenvalues as the primary object of interest. Instead, we ask what the full fitted operator can do transiently: how much amplification its geometry permits ($\eta$), how broadly it can respond across stride-related frequencies (harmonic resolvent profile and Q-factor), how symmetrically it routes perturbation pathways across limbs (spatial asymmetry), and how many effective directions it retains for distributing those responses (rank and participation ratio).

### Figure 1: Non-Normality Tracks Nonlinearity and Timescale Separation in Simulated Systems


```{julia}
#| label: fig-simulated
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Henrici departure from normality in simulated benchmark systems used to validate the interpretation of dynamical strain. (A) Stuart-Landau oscillator: as control parameter mu increases, limit-cycle amplitude grows and the fitted delay-embedded DMD operator becomes progressively more non-normal (higher eta). (B) FitzHugh-Nagumo oscillator: decreasing epsilon increases fast-slow timescale separation and produces a monotonic increase in eta. (C) Stuart-Landau oscillator with isochron shear: increasing shear parameter c raises eta, showing that amplitude-dependent phase warping is also captured as non-normal geometry in the fitted operator. (D) Two-parameter map over (mu, c), demonstrating that amplitude and shear combine to increase eta across broad regions of parameter space rather than at a narrow tuning point."
#| output: true

# _silent(() -> include("Figure 1.jl"))

include("Figure 1.jl")
plot(fig1, fmt=:png)
```

Simulated benchmark oscillators establish that dynamical strain ($\eta$) in a delay-embedded DMD operator rises with three properties of the underlying nonlinear flow: (A) limit-cycle amplitude (Stuart–Landau, increasing $\mu$), (B) timescale separation (FitzHugh–Nagumo, decreasing $\varepsilon$), and (C) isochron shear (Stuart–Landau with amplitude-dependent frequency, increasing $c$). Panel D extends this by mapping $\eta$ across the joint $(\mu, c)$ space, highlighting how amplitude and shear jointly amplify non-normality. These benchmarks validate $\eta$ as a graded index of dynamical complexity and confirm that the delay-embedded DMD framework reliably recovers the degree of nonlinearity in the generating system.

### Section 1: Able-Bodied Gait Defines a Speed-Robust Dynamical Baseline

#### Figure 2a: Dynamical Strain Is Speed-Invariant in Able-Bodied Walkers

```{julia}
#| label: fig-nonnormality-ab
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Dynamical strain (Henrici departure, η) as a function of walking speed for able-bodied walkers (n = 180 trials). Each point is one trial; thin line segments connect repeated speeds within the same participant. Strain remains approximately constant across the full speed range, indicating that the amount of non-normal encoding required to represent healthy walking does not change systematically with operating speed."
#| output: true

_silent(() -> include("Figure 2.jl"))
plot(fig2_ab_only; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
qAB  = quantile(filter(!isnan, df2[df2.group .== "AB", :henrici]), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
stAB = fig2_stats.corr["AB"]
rAB  = quantile(Float64.(filter(>(0), df2.rank[df2.group .== "AB"])), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])

println("""
Able-bodied trials maintain high dynamical strain (median \$\\eta\$ = $(round(qAB[2]; digits=2)), IQR [$(round(qAB[1]; digits=2)), $(round(qAB[3]; digits=2))]) that is not significantly correlated with walking speed (\$r\$ = $(round(stAB.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(stAB.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(stAB.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(stAB.p)); hierarchical bootstrap). The amount of non-normal encoding required to represent healthy walking stays approximately constant as speed changes -- the system accommodates a 7-fold range of walking speeds (30--220 cm/s) without requiring a qualitatively different coordination geometry. AB operators also retain the broadest effective dynamical subspace, with the highest SVD rank (median $(round(Int, rAB[2])) out of 66 possible dimensions).
""")
```

#### Figure 2b: Able-Bodied Frequency-Resolved Gain Is Broad and Approximately Speed-Invariant

```{julia}
#| label: fig-harmonic-ab-speed
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Frequency-resolved resolvent gain for able-bodied walkers, stratified by walking-speed bins (cool colours slower, warm colours faster). Each shaded curve shows the median gain swept across forcing frequency, with the interquartile range as the band. The curves remain broadly similar across speed bins, indicating that broadband resolvent responsiveness is a stable property of healthy walking rather than a speed-dependent one."
#| output: true

_silent(() -> include("Figure 3.jl"))
plot(ab_speed_curve_panel; fmt=:png)
```

#### Figure 2c: Able-Bodied Rank and Participation Ratio Are Stable Across Speed

```{julia}
#| label: fig-ab-dimensionality
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Dimensionality of able-bodied gait operators across walking speeds. Left: retained DMD rank vs walking speed, with semi-transparent within-subject trajectories. Right: Hankel participation ratio vs walking speed. Both measures remain approximately constant across the speed range, reinforcing that the healthy operator occupies a consistently broad dynamical subspace regardless of operating speed."
#| output: true

plot(pF1, pF2; layout=(1,2), size=(2*PUB_W, PUB_H), fmt=:png)
```

#### Figure 2d: Able-Bodied Coupling and Frequency Tuning vs Speed

```{julia}
#| label: fig-novel-ab-speed
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Able-bodied speed relationships for spatial coupling and frequency tuning. Left: inter-limb asymmetry (max/min cross-body resolvent gain) versus walking speed -- the ratio stays close to 1 with no systematic speed trend, indicating balanced bilateral coupling across the full speed range. Right: peak Q-factor versus walking speed -- a modest positive trend is present but the absolute scale remains low relative to stroke walkers (see Figure 3c)."
#| output: true

_silent(() -> include("Figure 4.jl"))
plot(fig4h; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
st_ab_asym_speed = fig4_stats.speed_corr["AB asym vs speed"]
st_ab_q_speed    = fig4_stats.speed_corr["AB qfactor vs speed"]
med_asym_ab = median(filter(!isnan, df4_sm[df4_sm.group .== "AB", :asym]))
med_q_ab    = median(filter(!isnan, df4_sm[df4_sm.group .== "AB", :qfactor]))

med_pr = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df3[df3.group .== g, :part_ratio])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)
med_decay = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df3[df3.group .== g, :decay_rate])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)

println("""
Across the full speed range, able-bodied gait is characterised by three consistent properties that together define the healthy coordination template. First, dynamical strain is speed-invariant: \$\\eta\$ shows no significant correlation with speed (above). Second, harmonic responsiveness is broad: the spectral profile decays at a moderate rate (median slope $(round(med_decay["AB"]; digits=2))) and the participation ratio of the Hankel singular spectrum remains high (median $(round(med_pr["AB"]; digits=2))), indicating that variance is distributed across many effective dynamical directions rather than concentrated in a few. Third, inter-limb coupling is approximately symmetric: the cross-body asymmetry ratio stays close to 1 (median $(round(med_asym_ab; digits=2))), with no clear speed dependence (\$r\$ = $(round(st_ab_asym_speed.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_ab_asym_speed.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_ab_asym_speed.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_ab_asym_speed.p))). Q-factor increases modestly with speed (\$r\$ = $(round(st_ab_q_speed.point; digits=2)), CI [$(round(st_ab_q_speed.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_ab_q_speed.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_ab_q_speed.p))), but this mild sharpening of stride-frequency tuning occurs on top of a coupling structure that stays balanced and a spectral profile that stays broad. These three stable properties -- speed-invariant strain, broadband harmonic support, and symmetric inter-limb coupling -- constitute the able-bodied reference template against which stroke is evaluated in the next section.
""")
```

### Section 2: Stroke Disrupts the Speed-Robust Able-Bodied Coordination Template

#### Figure 3a: Stroke Makes Dynamical Strain Speed-Dependent

```{julia}
#| label: fig-nonnormality-stroke
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Dynamical strain vs walking speed for all 299 trials, with able-bodied (steelblue) and combined stroke (brick-red) shown for direct comparison. Left: scatter with within-subject trajectories. Right: subject-level speed-sensitivity slopes (d η / d speed) for AB vs combined stroke. AB slopes cluster near zero; stroke slopes shift positive, confirming that the first property of the healthy template -- speed-invariance of dynamical strain -- is disrupted following stroke."
#| output: true

plot(fig2_stroke_vs_ab, fig2c_abvstroke; layout=(1,2), size=(2*PUB_W, PUB_H), fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
qAB  = quantile(filter(!isnan, df2[df2.group .== "AB", :henrici]), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
rAB  = quantile(Float64.(filter(>(0), df2.rank[df2.group .== "AB"])), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
qHF  = quantile(filter(!isnan, df2[df2.group .== "HF", :henrici]), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
qLF  = quantile(filter(!isnan, df2[df2.group .== "LF", :henrici]), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
rHF  = quantile(Float64.(filter(>(0), df2.rank[df2.group .== "HF"])), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])
rLF  = quantile(Float64.(filter(>(0), df2.rank[df2.group .== "LF"])), [0.25, 0.5, 0.75])

stAB   = fig2_stats.corr["AB"]
stHF   = fig2_stats.corr["HF"]
stLF   = fig2_stats.corr["LF"]
stHFAB = fig2_stats.matched_diff["HF-AB"]
stHFLF = fig2_stats.matched_diff["HF-LF"]
stLFAB = fig2_stats.matched_diff["LF-AB"]
st_combined = fig2_stats_combined.corr_stroke
st_comb_diff = fig2_stats_combined.matched_diff_stroke_ab

println("""
Across stroke walkers, dynamical strain increases significantly with walking speed (\$r\$ = $(round(st_combined.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_combined.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_combined.ci[2]; digits=2))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_combined.p))), in direct contrast to the near-zero speed dependence observed in AB walkers. Speed-matched comparisons (AB capped at \$\\leq\$ $(Int(speed_cap)) cm/s) confirm that stroke \$\\eta\$ differs significantly from AB (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_comb_diff.point; digits=3)), 95% CI [$(round(st_comb_diff.ci[1]; digits=3)), $(round(st_comb_diff.ci[2]; digits=3))], \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_comb_diff.p))). At the participant level, subject-level slopes (d\$\\eta\$/d speed) are close to zero in AB but shift clearly positive in stroke walkers, confirming that speed-dependence is a participant-level property, not merely a pooled-trial artefact.

Within the stroke group, higher-functioning walkers show the largest absolute \$\\eta\$ values (median $(round(qHF[2]; digits=2)), \$r\$(speed) = $(round(stHF.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(stHF.p))), whereas lower-functioning walkers show a smaller but still positive speed slope (median $(round(qLF[2]; digits=2)), \$r\$ = $(round(stLF.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(stLF.p))). Speed-matched comparisons show HF \$\\eta\$ exceeds LF (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(stHFLF.point; digits=3)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(stHFLF.p))) and trends higher than AB (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(stHFAB.point; digits=3)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(stHFAB.p))). Both stroke groups also occupy a lower-dimensional dynamical subspace than AB (median rank: AB $(round(Int, rAB[2])), HF $(round(Int, rHF[2])), LF $(round(Int, rLF[2])) out of 66 dimensions), so the speed-driven rise in \$\\eta\$ occurs in a more concentrated subspace -- fewer effective dimensions carrying more non-normal geometry.
""")
```

#### Figure 3b: Stroke Reshapes Harmonic Structure With Speed

```{julia}
#| label: fig-harmonic-stroke-speed
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Harmonic resolvent gain stratified by walking-speed bins for able-bodied (left) and combined stroke (right) walkers. Cool colours indicate slower bins, warm colours faster. AB profiles show modest speed-dependent variation and retain broadband support across bins. Stroke profiles show a more pronounced speed-dependent redistribution, with higher-speed bins producing a steeper fall-off from the fundamental -- the harmonic support that was stable in AB walkers narrows as stroke walkers walk faster."
#| output: true

plot(fig3_ab_stroke_speed; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
med_h1 = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df3[df3.group .== g, :gain_h1])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)
med_h2 = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df3[df3.group .== g, :gain_h2])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)
st_h1_hfab   = fig3_stats.matched_diff["gain_h1 HF-AB"]
st_decay_hfab = fig3_stats.matched_diff["decay HF-AB"]
st_decay_lfab = fig3_stats.matched_diff["decay LF-AB"]
st_decay_hflf = fig3_stats.matched_diff["decay HF-LF"]
st_rank_hfab  = fig3_stats.matched_diff["rank HF-AB"]
st_rank_lfab  = fig3_stats.matched_diff["rank LF-AB"]

println("""
The harmonic resolvent profiles quantify how broadly the fitted operator can respond across multiples of the stride frequency. In able-bodied walking the spectral profile is relatively broad (median decay slope $(round(med_decay["AB"]; digits=2))) and changes little across speed bins. Stroke does not alter that baseline in a single way. High-functioning stroke shows the steepest normalised fall-off (median slope $(round(med_decay["HF"]; digits=2))), significantly faster than AB (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_decay_hfab.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_decay_hfab.p))), whereas low-functioning stroke is closer to AB in shape ($(round(med_decay["LF"]; digits=2))), with no significant LF--AB difference (\$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_decay_lfab.p))). The paradox that HF has the highest dynamical strain yet the steepest normalised fall-off resolves as follows: HF also has by far the largest absolute fundamental gain (median $(round(med_h1["HF"] / 1e7; digits=1)) \$\\times 10^7\$ vs. $(round(med_h1["AB"] / 1e6; digits=1)) \$\\times 10^6\$ for AB; \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_h1_hfab.p))), so higher harmonics appear suppressed after normalisation even when their absolute magnitudes are not. The steep normalised fall-off in HF therefore reflects concentration of amplification at the stride rhythm, not the disappearance of higher-frequency responsiveness. LF, with both lower dimensionality (rank: LF vs AB \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_rank_lfab.point; digits=1)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_rank_lfab.p))) and lower absolute gains, responds weakly across the entire harmonic spectrum.
""")
```

#### Figure 3c: Stroke Q-Factor Increases Strongly With Speed

```{julia}
#| label: fig-qfactor-speed
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Peak Q-factor (stride-frequency tuning sharpness) vs walking speed. Left panel (reproduced from Section 1): able-bodied walkers show a modest positive speed trend with low absolute Q. Right panel: all stroke walkers (HF orange, LF crimson) show a much steeper speed-dependent increase in Q, indicating that faster walking is associated with progressively narrower stride-frequency locking. The dashed line is a pooled log-linear trend across all stroke subjects."
#| output: true

plot(fig4_stroke_q_speed; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
med_q = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df4_sm[df4_sm.group .== g, :qfactor])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)
st_q_hfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["qfactor HF-AB"]
st_q_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["qfactor LF-AB"]
st_q_hflf = fig4_stats.matched_diff["qfactor HF-LF"]
st_ab_q_speed = fig4_stats.speed_corr["AB qfactor vs speed"]

println("""
The Q-factor contrast illustrates clearly how speed affects stroke gait differently from healthy gait. In AB walkers, Q increases only modestly with speed (\$r\$ = $(round(st_ab_q_speed.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_ab_q_speed.p))), remaining at a low absolute level (median $(round(med_q["AB"]; digits=1))). Stroke walkers show a much steeper speed-dependent rise, with high-functioning stroke reaching the highest median Q ($(round(med_q["HF"]; digits=1)) vs AB $(round(med_q["AB"]; digits=1)); \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_q_hfab.point; digits=1)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_q_hfab.p))). Low-functioning stroke Q is not clearly elevated in speed-matched comparisons (\$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_q_lfab.p))), consistent with the picture that LF gait responds weakly at all frequencies rather than sharpening into a narrow resonant peak. The HF pattern -- highest dynamical strain, concentrated in fewer dimensions, locked to stride frequency -- contrasts with the LF pattern of broadly weakened responsiveness, and both differ from the AB template of stable broadband support across speeds.
""")
```

#### Figure 3d: Stroke Elevates Inter-Limb Coupling Asymmetry and Concentrates It on the Non-Paretic Knee

```{julia}
#| label: fig-novel-strips
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Speed-matched strip charts for spatial coupling and resonance metrics (AB, HF, LF). (A) Spatial resolvent asymmetry (max/min cross-body gain ratio), where 1 = perfectly symmetric bidirectional coupling. (B) Peak Q-factor. (C) Mean paretic-to-non-paretic resolvent gain. (D) Mean non-paretic-to-paretic gain. Each dot is one trial; black bars show median and IQR."
#| output: true

plot(fig4ab; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| label: fig-joint-summary
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Joint-level coupling summary metrics (speed-matched). (E) Knee-target asymmetry: log(hip→non-paretic knee / hip→paretic knee); positive values indicate hip coupling concentrated toward the non-paretic knee. (F) Non-paretic-knee directional bias: mean log[(hip→NP knee)/(NP knee→hip)]; positive values indicate net inward drive into the non-paretic knee. (G) Global pairwise asymmetry across all joint pairs. Both stroke groups show strong non-paretic-knee-centred asymmetry absent in AB walkers."
#| output: true

plot(fig4efg; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
med_asym = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df4_sm[df4_sm.group .== g, :asym])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)
st_asym_hfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["asym HF-AB"]
st_asym_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["asym LF-AB"]
st_dir_hf = fig4_stats.within_group["dir_bias HF"]
st_dir_lf = fig4_stats.within_group["dir_bias LF"]
st_npk_hfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["npk_inflow_asym HF-AB"]
st_npk_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["npk_inflow_asym LF-AB"]
st_npkbias_hf = fig4_stats.within_group["npk_bias HF"]
st_npkbias_lf = fig4_stats.within_group["npk_bias LF"]
st_npkbias_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["npk_bias LF-AB"]
st_global_lfab = fig4_stats.matched_diff["global_pair_asym LF-AB"]

println("""
The third property of the healthy template -- symmetric inter-limb coupling -- is clearly disrupted in stroke. The cross-body asymmetry ratio rises from near 1 in AB (median $(round(med_asym["AB"]; digits=2))) to $(round(med_asym["HF"]; digits=2)) in HF and $(round(med_asym["LF"]; digits=2)) in LF, with both stroke groups significantly elevated relative to AB (HF--AB \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_asym_hfab.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_asym_hfab.p)); LF--AB \$\\Delta\$ = $(round(st_asym_lfab.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_asym_lfab.p))). The directionality is clear: LF shows a significant paretic-to-non-paretic bias (mean log-ratio = $(round(st_dir_lf.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_dir_lf.p))), with HF trending similarly (\$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_dir_hf.p))).

At finer joint resolution, the asymmetry concentrates on a specific pathway rather than spreading diffusely. In both stroke groups, hip-originating coupling shifts toward the non-paretic knee (HF--AB \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_npk_hfab.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npk_hfab.p)); LF--AB \$\\Delta\$ = $(round(st_npk_lfab.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npk_lfab.p))), and both groups show strong directional inflow *into* the non-paretic knee rather than reciprocal exchange (HF: mean = $(round(st_npkbias_hf.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npkbias_hf.p)); LF: mean = $(round(st_npkbias_lf.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_npkbias_lf.p))). A broader summary of all pairwise joint asymmetries trends upward in LF (\$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_global_lfab.point; digits=2)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_global_lfab.p))), but the clearest and most consistent departure from the AB baseline is the routing of transient coupling through a non-paretic-knee-centred pathway.
""")
```

#### Figure 3e: Speed-Sensitivity of Dimensionality Correlates With Clinical Balance Function

```{julia}
#| label: fig-dimensionality-speed
#| fig-format: png
#| fig-cap: "Dimensionality diagnostics across groups. Left: retained DMD rank vs walking speed. Middle: Hankel participation ratio vs walking speed. Right: participation-ratio speed sensitivity (dPR/dspeed) vs Berg Balance Scale score across all stroke subjects; the dashed line is a single regression fit across the combined stroke cohort. Stroke subjects whose effective dimensionality varies more sharply with speed tend to have worse clinical balance function."
#| output: true

plot(fig3f; fmt=:png)
```

```{julia}
#| output: asis
st_rank_hfab = fig3_stats.matched_diff["rank HF-AB"]
st_rank_lfab = fig3_stats.matched_diff["rank LF-AB"]
st_pr_berg   = fig3_stats.clinical["pr_slope_vs_berg"]
st_pr_fugl   = fig3_stats.clinical["pr_slope_vs_fugl"]

pr_clin_text = isfinite(st_pr_berg.point) ?
  "Participation-ratio speed sensitivity correlates strongly with Berg Balance Scale score across all stroke subjects (r = $(round(st_pr_berg.point; digits=2)), 95% CI [$(round(st_pr_berg.ci[1]; digits=2)), $(round(st_pr_berg.ci[2]; digits=2))], p $(fmt_pvalue(st_pr_berg.p)); n = $(st_pr_berg.n)), so patients whose effective dimensionality varies more sharply with speed tend to have poorer functional balance. The same analysis against Fugl-Meyer is not clearly associated (r = $(round(st_pr_fugl.point; digits=2)), p $(fmt_pvalue(st_pr_fugl.p))), suggesting that this speed-sensitivity marker aligns more with functional balance capacity than with global motor impairment severity." :
  "Clinical score matching was unavailable for this run."

med_pr_vals = Dict(g => median(filter(!isnan, df3[df3.group .== g, :part_ratio])) for g in GROUP_ORDER)

println("""
AB operators retain the highest SVD rank (median rank: AB $(round(Int, quantile(Float64.(filter(>(0), df2.rank[df2.group .== "AB"])), 0.5)))) and the broadest Hankel participation ratio (median $(round(med_pr_vals["AB"]; digits=2))). Both are significantly reduced in stroke at matched speeds (HF--AB rank \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_rank_hfab.point; digits=1)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_rank_hfab.p)); LF--AB \$\\Delta\$median = $(round(st_rank_lfab.point; digits=1)), \$p\$ $(fmt_pvalue(st_rank_lfab.p))). Participation ratio is also highest in AB (median $(round(med_pr_vals["AB"]; digits=2))) compared to HF ($(round(med_pr_vals["HF"]; digits=2))) and LF ($(round(med_pr_vals["LF"]; digits=2))). $pr_clin_text
""")
```

### Supplemental Analyses

Exploratory analyses that support mechanism interpretation but are not required for the primary narrative are now designated as supplemental. These include sequential attenuation models, additional resolvent-breadth summaries, and gain-per-rank normalisation. Code for those analyses is centralised in `Supplemental.jl` (which wraps `Figure 5.jl`).

## Discussion

The results are most coherent when read as a baseline-and-breakdown story. Delay-embedded DMD first identifies a consistent able-bodied template for walking dynamics, and then shows that stroke does not simply shift gait along a single impairment axis but selectively breaks that template in different ways.

**Healthy gait defines a structured reference template.** In able-bodied walkers, three properties recur across analyses: dynamical strain is approximately speed-invariant, harmonic response remains broadly distributed across frequencies, and inter-limb coupling stays close to symmetric. Together these features imply that healthy walking is not merely stable in the narrow sense of resisting divergence; it is robustly organised across operating conditions, with enough dimensional richness to distribute transient amplification across multiple directions, frequencies, and joints.

**Stroke breaks that template in distinct, interpretable ways.** The central result is not just that stroke differs from AB, but *how* it differs. Both stroke groups lose the healthy speed-invariance of dynamical strain, indicating that the coordination strategy itself becomes speed-fragile. HF breaks the healthy template primarily by concentrating responsiveness sharply at stride frequency: it reaches the highest peak $\eta$ values and the highest Q-factors, but in fewer effective dimensions. LF breaks the template differently: it combines lower overall strain, weaker broadband responsiveness, and the strongest inter-limb asymmetry. Stroke therefore reorganises transient locomotor dynamics rather than uniformly degrading them.

**Relationship to healthy speed-manifold results.** These findings align with and extend @winner2024. Their healthy-only analysis showed that speed changes are structured and approximately linear in latent gait-signature space; our operator analysis recovers an analogous healthy regularity as near-zero AB speed dependence of $\eta$, broad harmonic support, and stable AB inter-limb symmetry across speeds. The key extension is that stroke does not merely shift subjects along the same speed axis: it changes the geometry of speed adaptation itself, with higher speed sensitivity of non-normal encoding, stronger spectral concentration, and directional coupling biases centered on the non-paretic knee.

As discussed in the Introduction, all three analysis frameworks--Floquet multipliers, Lyapunov exponents, and DMD--fit autonomous models to a system that is continuously driven and controlled [@dingwell2006; @ting2007]. The maximum Lyapunov exponent $\lambda_S$ remains the best-validated predictor of fall risk [@bruijn2013], but as a scalar divergence rate of an autonomous reconstruction it cannot distinguish intrinsic instability from neural driving, nor can it capture the directional or frequency-dependent structure of transient dynamics. Dynamical strain, by indexing how much non-normal eigenvector geometry the autonomous linear model must deploy to encode the driven, nonlinear data, captures a complementary dimension of locomotor organisation. Where $\lambda_S$ summarises the dominant instability direction, the resolvent structure maps the full landscape of transient amplification across frequencies and spatial channels.

**Fundamental-dominant amplification and dimensionality shape spectral organisation.** The harmonic resolvent profiles initially appear paradoxical: HF has the highest dynamical strain yet the steepest normalised harmonic fall-off. The resolution is that the normalised curves are dominated by what happens at h=1. HF produces a very large absolute fundamental response, so after dividing by h=1 its higher harmonics appear strongly suppressed even when their absolute magnitudes remain comparable to AB's. The steep normalised fall-off in HF therefore reflects concentrated amplification at the stride rhythm, not a simple disappearance of higher-frequency responsiveness.

Dimensionality completes this picture. AB operators retain the highest SVD rank and the broadest participation ratio, meaning transient amplification can be distributed across more directions and frequencies, supporting the broad harmonic bandwidth observed in Figure 3. HF operators concentrate their high dynamical strain into fewer dimensions, yielding the “narrow but intense” spectral profile -- sharp stride-frequency tuning rather than broad harmonic support. LF operators, with both lower dimensionality and lower $\eta$, respond weakly across all frequencies. From the perspective of @wu2021, the non-normality they identify as a challenge for DMD convergence here becomes a graded diagnostic: AB dynamics demand broad spectral coverage from the autonomous linear model; HF dynamics demand acute coverage at one dominant frequency; LF dynamics demand the least coverage of all.

**Asymmetric coupling focused on the non-paretic knee.** The directionality of inter-limb coupling provides perhaps the most clinically informative signature. At the coarse inter-limb level, LF shows clear paretic-dominant cross-body drive, with HF trending in the same direction. At finer joint resolution, however, the most robust effect is even more specific: hip-knee coupling becomes concentrated on the non-paretic knee, and both stroke groups show strong directional bias into that knee from the hips. Rather than the intact side simply compensating for the impaired side in a diffuse manner, the operator structure suggests that stroke channels transient coupling through a knee-centered pathway on the non-paretic limb. That picture remains consistent with the clinical observation that the “good” leg works harder during stroke gait, but it localises the asymmetry to a specific operator-level route through which perturbations are absorbed and redistributed.

**Relationship to existing stability measures.** @bruijn2013 evaluated gait stability measures against four validity criteria: construct validity, convergent validity, predictive validity, and discriminant validity. Floquet multipliers showed poor construct validity for human gait--not only because strict periodicity is violated, but because they require prohibitively large datasets (>10,000 strides in @revzen2008 vs. ~15–50 strides in typical human trials), demand a reliable phase estimator [@revzen2008; @revzen2012], and are acutely noise-sensitive. $\lambda_S$ showed good predictive validity but limited discriminant validity between populations, and as @dingwell2006 demonstrated, the coexistence of positive $\lambda_S$ (local divergence) with orbital stability reflects the driven nature of the system rather than a genuine autonomous instability. The operator-level metrics introduced here show substantial discriminant validity, with the strongest and most consistent separations in AB-vs-stroke contrasts, and capture facets of locomotor organisation--transient richness, frequency bandwidth, directional coupling--that no single scalar can represent. Like DMD itself, these metrics inherit the autonomous-model assumption, but they make the demands of that assumption visible: $\eta$ indexes how much non-normal eigenvector geometry the linear model must deploy to encode the underlying dynamics. Establishing predictive validity for fall risk and convergent validity with $\lambda_S$ are natural directions for future work.

**Methodological considerations.** Our approach builds on the DMD framework of @schmid2010 and @tu2014, with delay embedding following @takens1981. @wu2021 showed that DMD applied to limit-cycle data faces challenges from Krylov space closure and non-normality-amplified estimation errors, and characterised performance via a dataset metric (number of initial conditions $\times$ trajectory length). Our dataset of 299 trials with 1500 time points each provides substantial coverage; the stability enforcement (eigenvalue projection inside the unit disk) and SVD truncation (retaining >99% variance) guard against the most severe numerical pathologies they identify. Nevertheless, several limitations apply. The analysis uses a single treadmill dataset; overground walking may produce different operator structure. The choice of embedding lag ($\tau = 10$) is motivated by stride-period considerations at 100 Hz but has not been exhaustively optimised. The linear operator is a surrogate--it captures the Koopman-linearised dynamics faithfully only within the delay-embedded subspace, and nonlinear effects beyond this approximation are not modelled. Speed-matched comparisons cap AB trials at the maximum HF walking speed (127 cm/s); cross-group comparisons at higher speeds are not possible because AB is the only group that regularly walks above this threshold.

In additional lag-sensitivity checks, our core findings remained stable across a practical range of $\tau$ values as long as the delay embedding still captured roughly one full cycle and preserved sufficient effective rank. The main failure mode was not a specific $\tau$ value per se, but dimensionality impoverishment when delays became too short to represent cycle structure.

**Clinical implications.** Collectively, these results suggest that post-stroke locomotor impairment is not a scalar degradation but a structured reorganisation of transient dynamics. Restoring walking speed or step symmetry may not restore the dimensional richness and robust dynamical encoding (speed-invariant $\eta$) that characterise healthy gait. The group-specific signatures identified here--speed-fragile encoding concentrated in few dimensions in HF, rising encoding demand with weak absolute responsiveness in LF, and knee-centered coupling asymmetry that is strongest in LF--point toward different rehabilitation targets for different functional levels. Interventions that broaden the transient repertoire, such as perturbation-based balance training, multi-frequency rhythmic cueing, or assistive devices that modulate inter-limb coupling directionality, may prove more effective than approaches targeting kinematic outcomes alone.

**Outlook.** The present work demonstrates that non-normality in data-driven linear operators is not merely a numerical nuisance but a window into the transient organisation of biological movement. A direct next test of the dimensionality hypothesis is to quantify whether rank mediates spectral breadth: regress decay slope and Q-factor on group with and without rank, and bootstrap within-group partial correlations between rank and (i) decay slope, (ii) h2/h1, and (iii) Q. If HF effects attenuate after conditioning on rank, that would support the “concentrated low-dimensional amplification” mechanism. Future extensions--incorporating residual DMD for rigorous spectral convergence guarantees [@colbrook2021], measure-theoretic Koopman formulations [@mezic2005; @mezic2013], or hybrid embeddings that combine delay coordinates with nonlinear observables--promise richer operator-level phenotypes for clinical stratification and closed-loop control.

## Methods

### Dataset

We analyse 299 treadmill walking trials drawn from the open dataset of @winner2023. Each trial comprises 1500 time points (15 s at 100 Hz) of six kinematic channels: bilateral hip, knee, and ankle sagittal-plane joint angles. Trials are partitioned by metadata into able-bodied (AB, $n = 180$; speed range 30–220 cm/s), high-functioning stroke (HF, $n = 65$; 40–127 cm/s), and low-functioning stroke (LF, $n = 54$; 22–60 cm/s). All channels are z-scored within each trial prior to analysis, so that operator properties reflect coordination structure rather than trivial amplitude differences between subjects or joints.

### Delay-Embedded Hankel DMD

Let $y_t \in \mathbb{R}^6$ denote the six joint angles at time $t$. We construct a delay-embedded state by stacking $\tau = 10$ successive observations,
$$
x_t = \begin{bmatrix} y_t \\ y_{t+1} \\ \vdots \\ y_{t+\tau} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{6(\tau+1)} = \mathbb{R}^{66},
$$
and assemble those states into a Hankel matrix
$$
H = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 & x_2 & \cdots & x_{T-\tau} \end{bmatrix}.
$$
This embedding spans approximately one stride period at 100 Hz sampling, so each state contains both instantaneous posture and a short temporal history of the gait cycle. We then split $H$ into input and output matrices,
$$
X = H_{:,1:end-1}, \qquad Y = H_{:,2:end},
$$
and fit a reduced DMD operator by singular-value projection:
$$
X = U \Sigma V^*, \qquad \tilde{A} = U_r^* Y V_r \Sigma_r^{-1},
$$
where $r$ retains singular values larger than $10^{-6}$ times the leading singular value (typically >99% of the variance). To avoid unstable numerical fits dominating downstream resolvent calculations, eigenvalues of $\tilde{A}$ with modulus greater than 1 are projected back to the unit circle. All operator-level analyses are then performed on this reduced fitted system.

Biomechanically, the point of the delay embedding is that gait is not well represented by a single posture snapshot. The embedded state carries phase and short-timescale context, allowing a simple linear operator to encode how joint patterns evolve across a stride. The operator is therefore not interpreted as a literal physiological mechanism, but as a compact surrogate whose geometry reveals how much transient structure the underlying gait pattern forces the model to carry.

Sensitivity analyses over embedding lag show that the main group-level contrasts are robust provided the embedding captures approximately a full stride cycle and the retained subspace is not dimensionality-impoverished; performance degrades primarily when lags are too short to capture cycle structure.

### Operator-Level Metrics

**Dynamical strain (Henrici departure from normality).** We quantify global non-normality as
$$
\eta = \frac{\|A^*A - AA^*\|_2}{\|A\|_2^2},
$$
where $\|\cdot\|_2$ denotes the spectral norm. A normal operator has orthogonal eigenvectors and therefore $\eta = 0$; larger $\eta$ indicates stronger non-orthogonality and greater potential for transient amplification [@henrici1962; @trefethen2005]. In biomechanical terms, $\eta$ measures how much directional, transiently amplifying structure the linear model must deploy to reproduce the gait waveform. High $\eta$ does not mean the walker is simply "unstable"; rather, it means the fitted operator must work harder geometrically to encode the observed coordination.

**Harmonic resolvent gain.** For a complex forcing frequency $z = e^{i 2\pi f / f_{\mathrm{samp}}}$, the resolvent norm
$$
\|(zI - A)^{-1}\|
$$
measures how strongly the operator would amplify a periodic input at frequency $f$. We first identify the stride frequency $f_s$ as the dominant peak above 0.3 Hz in the resolvent gain swept around the unit circle. We then evaluate gains at stride harmonics,
$$
G_h = \left\| \left(e^{i 2\pi h f_s / f_{\mathrm{samp}}} I - A \right)^{-1} \right\|, \qquad h=1,\ldots,5.
$$
Mathematically, these gains summarize the frequency-dependent transient responsiveness of the fitted operator. Biomechanically, they ask whether the coordination structure can respond broadly across multiple timescales or whether it is concentrated sharply at the stride rhythm.

**Spectral decay rate.** To summarize the shape of the harmonic profile independent of overall scale, we normalize by the fundamental and fit the slope of $\log(G_h/G_1)$ against harmonic number. More negative slopes indicate faster fall-off away from the fundamental. Biomechanically, this distinguishes a broad transient repertoire from a narrowly stride-locked one.

**Peak Q-factor.** We quantify the sharpness of the stride-frequency response by
$$
Q = \frac{f_s}{\Delta f},
$$
where $\Delta f$ is the half-power bandwidth around the stride-frequency peak. Higher $Q$ means narrower tuning. Biomechanically, high $Q$ indicates a system that responds strongly at stride frequency but less flexibly to neighboring frequencies.

**Spatial resolvent asymmetry.** We map the stride-frequency resolvent back to the full delay-embedded joint space,
$$
R(z_s) = U (z_s I - \tilde{A})^{-1} U^*, \qquad z_s = e^{i 2\pi f_s / f_{\mathrm{samp}}},
$$
and extract cross-body blocks corresponding to one limb driving the other. If $G_{12}$ and $G_{21}$ denote the operator norms of the two cross-body directions, the asymmetry index is
$$
\mathrm{Asym} = \frac{\max(G_{12}, G_{21})}{\min(G_{12}, G_{21})}.
$$
An asymmetry of 1 implies perfectly balanced bidirectional influence; larger values imply that perturbation pathways are routed preferentially in one direction. At finer scale we also compute signed log-ratios between joint-to-joint gains, especially around hip-to-knee pathways. Biomechanically, these measures ask where perturbations are absorbed and whether one limb or joint acts as a preferred sink or conduit.

**Dimensionality metrics: retained rank and participation ratio.** The retained DMD rank $r$ counts how many singular directions survive truncation. We complement that with a continuous participation ratio computed from the Hankel singular values $\sigma_i$:
$$
\mathrm{PR} = \frac{\left(\sum_i \sigma_i^2\right)^2}{\sum_i \sigma_i^4}.
$$
High rank or participation ratio implies that variance and transient responsiveness are distributed across many effective dimensions; low values imply concentration into a smaller dynamical subspace. Biomechanically, these metrics index whether the walker uses a rich, distributed coordination repertoire or a narrower, more concentrated one.

All quantities are computed trial-by-trial. Speed-matched comparisons cap AB trials at the maximum HF walking speed (127 cm/s) to control for the confound that AB subjects walk faster on average.

### Statistical Inference

Because each participant contributes multiple trials at different walking speeds, inferential comparisons use a hierarchical bootstrap rather than treating trials as independent replicates. Within each group, subjects are resampled with replacement, and then that subject's trials are resampled with replacement within the bootstrap draw. This preserves the nested subject/trial structure while accommodating unequal trial counts per subject. For between-group contrasts we bootstrap differences in medians; for speed-dependence we bootstrap Pearson correlations; and for directional quantities expressed as log-ratios we bootstrap the mean log-ratio. We report percentile 95% confidence intervals and two-sided bootstrap p-values computed from the fraction of bootstrap samples on either side of zero. Unless otherwise noted, cross-group inference is reported on the speed-matched subset.

## Data accessibility

The gait recordings analysed here are openly available through the datasets described by @winner2023. The present analyses use those open-source records without restriction.

## Code accessibility

All code used for data processing, model fitting, statistics and figure generation will be available at [github.com/michaelhess17/Gait_NonNormality_Paper](https://github.com/michaelhess17/Gait_NonNormality_Paper). If the repository is migrated to the Berman Lab organization, it will retain the same repository name.

## Authors' contributions

M.H. conceived and implemented the analysis workflow, performed the full computational analysis, generated all figures and led manuscript drafting. G.B. contributed to study ideation, interpretation, supporting investigations, and manuscript editing and revision. Both authors approved the final manuscript.

## Competing interests

We declare we have no competing interests.

## Funding

This work was supported by the National Institute on Aging (NIH R01AG082039) and a Simons-Emory International Consortium on Motor Control Micro-accelerator grant.
